### Post by ltmon on 2006-06-22
Sorry for not replying... I lose threads sometimes!

The only part that looks really odd to me is the oft repeated line in the Xorg log:
```

drmOpenByBusid: drmOpenMinor returns -1023
drmOpenDevice: node name is /dev/dri/card254
drmOpenDevice: open result is -1, (No such device)
drmOpenDevice: open result is -1, (No such device)
drmOpenDevice: Open failed

```

You seem to have the proprietry ATI drivers (fglrx) set up.  Does it make a difference if you turn these off?  Let me know if you don't know how to do this.

L.

### Post by ltmon on 2006-06-24
At least we can rule out one thing as the cause.

I'm into the guesswork area now, but have a look at /var/log/acpid (post it for me to look at if necessary).  Possibly your laptop is firing off acpi events unexpectedly or something like that.  It might account for the change in behaviour when you're holding a key down.

Also have a look at the system monitor (ctrl-Esc).  Sort alternately by "User %", "System %" and "VM Size".  See if anything at the top of the list looks out of place.  You can also right click on kicker (the panel) and add an applet called "System Guard".  It will tell you your CPU and memory load.  Does it look highly loaded in any way?

Cheers,

L.

---

### Post by bluedeep on 2006-06-24
You can find here acpid: [http://terminet.ns0.it/acpid](http://terminet.ns0.it/acpid)

I've looked on system monitor, but I didn't find anything out of place (Xorg and Ksysguard on top with low percentage around 1-2%)

Cheers,
bd

---

### Post by ltmon on 2006-06-24
Well I think we found it!!!  Now to fix it.

There is a continuous acpi event being fired, as though the lid was being closed repeatedly.  This is calling the actions in /etc/acpi/lid.sh.  I think that script is trying to blank your screen.

It's failing, with something about DCOP (a standard part of KDE, which should be running).  You can check this by typing into a terminal:
```

ps aux | grep dcopserver

```
Which should output something like:
```

ltmon     4661  0.0  0.4  24700  2120 ?        S    13:14   0:02 dcopserver [kdeinit] --nosid
ltmon    29914  0.0  0.1   2896   832 pts/1    S+   18:07   0:00 grep dcop

```

I think getting rid of the acpi event altogether is the best way to fix.  There is a testing page for the laptop at [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/LaptopTestingTeam/AcerAspire5514WLMi](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/LaptopTestingTeam/AcerAspire5514WLMi).  There's no indication there of that laptop having a problem like this.

Have you gotten the latest BIOS for your laptop model?

L.

### Post by ltmon on 2006-06-27
[QUOTE=bluedeep]What do you think about framebuffer? Can it make this problem?

Cheers,
bd[/QUOTE]

Not as far as I'm aware, but I might be wrong.

Another place to look for repeating errors: /home/bluedeep/.xsession-errors

Maybe that will have a clue, but I still think it's the ACPI thing.  You can test this with the following code:
```

sudo /etc/init.d/acpid stop

```

If the problem is fixed then acpi is definately the culprit.  Of course you now won't have any acpi events, so it's not a proper solution.

L.

### Post by micranet on 2006-06-29
